children.text: Yeah, we had all that silence, now. So here we are revisiting Bent. Well, maybe I'll back up. We first did Bent in 2007, I think. It is now 2018, and I'm wondering a little bit about what's happened since then.

11 years. 11 years. I think since the first time we first started working on Bent, I just became interested, in almost kind of a nostalgic way about remembering my experimental college youth, and the sensations that came from the different altered states, I would really say, of being high, and when that was in an interesting place, and then when it was in a not so interesting place. I think at that time, there was quite a bit of ...

So I think at that time, the 60s, 70s, there was a lot of permission as maybe a counter culture, or bohemian culture, or however you would like at that in terms of going outside of the things that you knew, to experience other realms, other states of being. I think since that time, there is a more broad use of drugs, in not a good way, through the opiod epidemic. So, heroin and crack, those things were in the black community, and they were criminalized in the black community.

Now what has happened is that a lot of these things are legal, and they've gone beyond black communities and poor communities, out into all parts of the fabric of this country and beyond. And so now for me, it takes on a really dangerous ... somebody going to the edge now has more of a sense of danger and tragedy, like Prince. I really felt that when he died. I felt it. It was just something that I was shaken to my core about.

So, I kinda feel that now. I still feel that now as I'm looking at the piece, and what is that place where you're like, âEverything's okay, it's good. I'm experimenting,â and then that place where it's even beyond not okay. It's into something horribly tragic. And particularly, the ways in suburban middle class, what we call the inner city, rural, all the ways that these communities are affected that people thought, âWell that's that problem over there. We don't have to worry about it. It's over there. It's in the black community. It's with poor people. We don't have to really worry about it.â But now we see, something that affects one of us is eventually gonna have a negative effect on all of us.

When you were watching this piece ... we were talking a little bit about this ... what is it like to see something that you made, that usually reflects a time period in your life, or a moment, or a type of interest? You spoke to that a little bit, but what is it like to see that then and to consider ... A lot of people don't even wanna redo dances, so what is it like to have to come back to this, to see it, and then to think about re-staging it, or re-working it?

I love history, and so even though it's uncomfortable to maybe go back, I think I said that it's like reading old love letters from somebody that you broke up with years ago and you're like ... Now you're reading them over and over and over again. Partly, it's like ...

Part of it is that I'm not there anymore as a choreographer, but historically, I was. And so, I can't deny, or I don't want to deny this was a very specific time in my aesthetic in choreographic development. And so, I do feel an obligation to that, and at the same time, nothing exists in a vacuum. So, I enjoy the process of what makes it more relevant now, what activates it for this time without completely redoing the work.
How would you say things have shifted for you aesthetically or choreographically in the last 10 years?

In some ways choreographically, I'm almost closer back to where I started. That is in the very early ... A lot of my early work, I wasn't interested in modern dance form, as modern dance or ballet form as we know it, so I was constantly saying to the dancers, âDon't point your feet, don't straighten your legs, don't have that pulled up look. I want it to look more natural, I want it to look more rooted, and more connected to everyday life.â

I think I became interested in a period of time like, okay I've got these dancers with a certain degree of training. How do I ... Let me restate that. I began working with dancers who have a certain kind of facility that was maybe more like a repertoire company. I became interested in like, okay what would it be like to begin to work with that kind of form so it's more, you see the feet pointed, you see a certain ...

set: 2018 Jawole Willa Jo Zollar Interview (2 of 4)
